title: Intel Antes Up $1B on Success of Itanium Line (USA Today)
author: Michelle Kessler (USA Today)
abstract: Michelle Kessler's USA Today story (1 March 2002) on Intel's Itanium line and the McKinley successor preview. Reports $1 billion six-year HP+Intel Itanium investment, $1000-$4000 chip pricing, and the chicken-and-egg software adoption problem caused by Itanium's 64-bit architecture incompatibility with 32-bit Intel. Quotes Aberdeen Group's Peter Kastner: 'Intel and H-P are holding a party, and nobody's coming.' Notes Intel's reported backup plan to develop a 64-bit version of its 32-bit processor line (foreshadowing AMD64/x86-64 response and Intel's eventual EM64T). Cites HP+Compaq merger as additional Itanium-volume risk.
importance_rationale: Marquee Itanium-criticism quote from Kastner with the now-iconic 'holding a party and nobody's coming' line.
relevance_rationale: Direct Kastner Aberdeen quote in major national newspaper on a strategic Intel platform; pairs with later Itanium post-mortem coverage in the archive.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's 2002 'nobody's coming' framing was decisively vindicated — Itanium peaked at modest enterprise volumes, AMD64 (and Intel's reactive EM64T) decisively won the 64-bit-extension architectural battle, and Itanium was effectively wound down by 2017 (Kittson final SKU); story also flagged the HP-Compaq merger consolidation risk that further reduced Itanium server volumes.
